title: ITU and Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computing: A Single-Axiom Derivation of Optimal QECC Architectures (v1.0.0)
description:
We apply the Information-Theoretic Unification (ITU) framework (Terada 2026, DOI 10.5281/zenodo.20109210) to fault-tolerant quantum computing. Phase 5 of ITU established that bulk locality in AdS/CFT is mathematically equivalent to a quantum error correction code (QECC); here we exploit the reverse direction — using the ITU axiom δS = δ<K> to derive optimal QECC architectures for practical quantum computation.

This is a Tier 1 application paper, complementary to the Tier 0 core ITU theory. It is the first in a planned series of focused application papers (ITU + AI, ITU + cancer biology, ITU + economics, etc.), each as an independent Zenodo deposit linked via isSupplementTo relations to the core ITU concept DOI.

Phase 43 (this release): verifies the [[5,1,3]] perfect quantum code — the discrete realization of bulk locality in AdS3/CFT2 (Pastawski-Yoshida-Harlow-Preskill HaPPY code building block) — as a complete fault-tolerant primitive:

  • 4 stabilizers, code distance d = 3
  • All 15 single-qubit Pauli errors yield distinct syndromes
  • Decoder lookup table of size 16 (24) correctly maps every syndrome to its corrective operation
  • Numerical pseudo-threshold: encoded logical error rate is below the bare-qubit rate at physical error rates up to 25%

Forthcoming phases in this paper:
Phase 44 — ITU-motivated surface code variants and threshold comparison
Phase 45 — Magic-state distillation in the ITU framework
Phase 46 — NISQ-era hardware applications and concrete predictions

Central thesis: the same single information-theoretic axiom that yields emergent spacetime (ITU Phases 1-32), life (Phases 33-39), and consciousness (Phases 41-42) also yields practical quantum-computing primitives. ITU is not merely a theoretical TOE but a constructive framework that can be applied to engineering problems.

Includes Python numerical experiment, 4-panel figure, theory document, and JSON summary. Total runtime ~10 seconds on a modern laptop.
